howItWorks.intro: Your protein needs depend on your body weight and activity level. This calculator provides a personalized daily target with per-meal breakdown and real food equivalents.
rules: Sedentary: 0.36g per pound — the bare minimum for health
Muscle gain: 0.7-1.0g per pound — the range supported by research for hypertrophy
Cutting: 1.0-1.2g per pound — higher protein preserves muscle during a deficit
howItWorks.outro: Spreading protein evenly across meals (20-40g per meal) maximizes muscle protein synthesis. The total daily amount matters most, but meal distribution can provide a small additional benefit.

faq.question: How much protein do I need to build muscle?
faq.answer: Research consistently shows 0.7-1.0 grams per pound of body weight (1.6-2.2g per kg) is optimal for muscle building. Going higher than 1g per pound provides no additional benefit for most people. A 170-lb person should aim for 120-170g per day.

faq.question: Can I eat too much protein?
faq.answer: For healthy individuals, high protein intake (up to 1.5g per pound) has not been shown to cause kidney damage or other health issues in research. However, there's no benefit to going much beyond 1g per pound for muscle building. Excess protein is simply used for energy.

faq.question: What are the best protein sources?
faq.answer: Chicken breast (31g per 4 oz), eggs (6g each), Greek yogurt (15-20g per cup), fish (25-30g per 4 oz), lean beef (28g per 4 oz), tofu (20g per cup), and whey protein (25g per scoop) are all excellent sources with high bioavailability.

faq.question: Does it matter when I eat protein?
faq.answer: Total daily intake matters most. However, spreading protein across 3-5 meals with 20-40g per meal optimizes muscle protein synthesis. The anabolic window after exercise is longer than once thought — eating protein within 2-3 hours of training is sufficient.

faq.question: Should I use body weight or lean mass for calculation?
faq.answer: This calculator uses total body weight, which works well for most people. If you're significantly overweight (above 30% body fat), using lean body mass or goal weight may give a more practical target to avoid excessively high protein amounts.

faq.question: Do I need more protein when losing weight?
faq.answer: Yes. Higher protein intake (1.0-1.2g per pound) during a calorie deficit helps preserve muscle mass and keeps you feeling full. Research shows that people who eat more protein during a cut retain significantly more muscle compared to lower protein groups.

sections.title: What Is the Protein Intake Calculator?
sections.body: The Protein Intake Calculator is a free online tool designed for athletes and fitness enthusiasts who need quick, accurate calculations in the fitness and training space. By entering your units, body weight, goal, you get instant results including daily protein, per meal, range. No formulas to memorize, no spreadsheets to build — just enter your numbers and get the answer in seconds. Whether you're a beginner or experienced professional, this calculator saves you time and eliminates guesswork.

sections.title: Why This Calculation Matters
sections.body: Getting daily protein right can make the difference between success and costly mistakes. In fitness and training, small errors compound quickly. Manual calculations are error-prone and time-consuming, especially under pressure. This calculator applies proven formulas used by athletes and fitness enthusiasts worldwide, giving you confidence that your numbers are correct. Use it to optimize your training with precision and avoid common pitfalls that trip up beginners.

sections.title: When Should You Use This Calculator?
sections.body: This tool is most useful when you know your units and need to find the right daily protein. It's also great for quick estimates before committing to a decision, and to double-check manual calculations or professional quotes, and when comparing different scenarios side by side. Bookmark this page and come back whenever you need a fast, reliable answer — the calculator is always free and requires no signup.

howToSteps.title: Enter Your Units
howToSteps.description: Start by entering your units — this is the primary input for the calculation.

howToSteps.title: Fill In Additional Details
howToSteps.description: Complete the remaining fields: body weight, goal, meals per day. Each value refines the calculation for greater accuracy.

howToSteps.title: Click Calculate
howToSteps.description: Hit the Calculate button to run the numbers. Results appear instantly below.

howToSteps.title: Review Your Results
howToSteps.description: Check your daily protein, per meal, range. Use these figures to inform your next decision or compare against alternative scenarios.

tips: Double-check your units before calculating — even small input errors can significantly change your results.
Run the calculator with different values to compare scenarios and find the optimal approach for your situation.
Pay attention to both daily protein and per meal — they work together to give you the full picture.
Bookmark this page for quick access next time you need to optimize your training.
If you're unsure about your meals per day, start with a conservative estimate and adjust from there.
